This chapter gives a clear indication of the mercy God has in store for Israel in the latter days. This passage refers to the prophetic warnings of the last two chapters, which have been mainly fulfilled in the destruction of Jerusalem by the Romans, and in their dispersion to the present day; and there is no doubt that the prophetic promise contained in these verses still remains to come to pass. The Jewish nation will in some future period, perhaps not very distant, be converted to the faith of Christ; and many think they will again be settled in the land of Canaan. The language here used is largely absolute promises; not merely a conditional engagement, but declaring an event that will assuredly take place. For the Lord himself engages to “circumcise their hearts;” and when regenerating grace has removed corrupt nature, and Divine love has supplanted the love of sin, they certainly will reflect, repent, return to God, and obey him; and he will rejoice in doing them good. The change that will be wrought upon them will not be only outward, or consisting in mere opinions; it will reach to their souls. It will produce in them an utter hatred of all sin, and a fervent love to God, as their reconciled God in Christ Jesus; they will love him with all their hearts, and with all their soul. They are very far from this state of mind at present, but so were the murderers of the Lord Jesus on the day of Pentecost; who yet in one hour were converted unto God. So it will be in the day of God’s power; a nation will be born in a day; the Lord will hasten it in his time. As a conditional promise this passage belongs to all people, not to Israel only; it assures us that the greatest sinners, if they repent and are converted, will have their sins pardoned, and be restored to God’s favor.

The law is not too high for you. It is not only known afar off; it is not confined to men of learning. It is written in your books, made plain so that anyone who runs may read it. It is in your mouth, in the language commonly used by you, in which you may hear it read and talk about it among your children. It is delivered so that it is understandable even to the least intelligent person. This is especially true of the gospel of Christ, to which the apostle applies it. But the word is near us, and Christ in that word; so if we believe with our heart that the promises of the Messiah are fulfilled in our Lord Jesus, and confess them with our mouth, then we have Christ with us.

What could be said that is more moving, and more likely to make deep and lasting impressions? Every person wishes to obtain life and good, and to escape death and evil; he desires happiness, and dreads misery. So great is the compassion of the Lord, that he has favored people with such knowledge of good and evil by his word as will make them forever happy, if it is not their own fault. Let us hear the sum of the whole matter: If they and theirs love God, and serve him, they will live and be happy. If they or theirs turn from God, desert his service, and worship other gods, that will certainly be their ruin. There never was, since the fall of man, more than one way to heaven; which is marked out in both Testaments, though not with equal clearness. Moses meant that same way of acceptance, which Paul more plainly described; and Paul’s words mean the same obedience, on which Moses more fully treated. In both Testaments the good and right way is brought near, and plainly revealed to us.
